content.text: Masked language modelling (MLM) is a self-supervised pretraining objective introduced by BERT. During training, a random subset of input tokens is hidden ("masked") and the model must predict their original values from the surrounding context. Because the model can see
simultaneously — there is no causal mask — it builds deep bidirectional representations of each token.

This contrasts with causal language modelling (CLM) where each token can only attend to previous tokens. MLM is better for tasks that require understanding the full sentence (classification, question answering, named-entity recognition); CLM is better for generation.

attrs.alt: MLM masking procedure (80/10/10 split), bidirectional attention on [MASK] token, comparison table MLM vs CLM vs Seq2Seq

content.text: BERT masks 15% of tokens per sequence. Of those selected tokens, the replacement is not always
— the 80/10/10 split prevents the model from learning to only predict at positions where it sees
— forces the model to maintain a representation for every token position, not just masked ones.
— forces the model to produce useful representations even when input matches the original.

— unmasked tokens contribute nothing to the gradient. This makes MLM less data-efficient per token than CLM (which predicts 100% of positions), which is why BERT and its descendants need more compute to achieve comparable understanding depth.

import torch
import random

def mask_tokens(input_ids, vocab_size, mask_token_id,
                mask_prob=0.15, seed=42):
    """
    Apply BERT-style masking.
    Returns (masked_ids, labels) where labels = -100 at non-masked positions.
    """
    rng = random.Random(seed)
    labels = input_ids.clone()           # original tokens — the targets
    masked = input_ids.clone()

    for i, token in enumerate(input_ids.tolist()):
        if rng.random() < mask_prob:
            r = rng.random()
            if r < 0.80:
                masked[i] = mask_token_id     # [MASK]
            elif r < 0.90:
                masked[i] = rng.randint(0, vocab_size - 1)  # random token
            # else: keep original (10%)
        else:
            labels[i] = -100              # -100 → ignored by CrossEntropyLoss

    return masked, labels


# Example (single sequence)
vocab_size = 30522
mask_id    = 103   # BERT's [MASK] id
tokens     = torch.tensor([101, 1996, 4248, 2829, 4419, 14523, 2015, 102])
masked, labels = mask_tokens(tokens, vocab_size, mask_id)
print("masked:", masked.tolist())
print("labels:", labels.tolist())   # -100 at unmasked positions
Unlike a causal LM, a masked LM uses the

with no lower-triangular mask. Every token attends to every other token in the sequence. The token at the masked position can draw information from tokens to its left and right simultaneously — which is what makes MLM representations powerful for understanding tasks.
To predict the masked token the model stacks a linear head on top of the transformer output at the masked position and computes a cross-entropy loss against the original vocabulary item.
Implementing MLM with a Pretrained BERT

from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertForMaskedLM
import torch

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model     = BertForMaskedLM.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model.eval()

# Manually mask a token
text       = "The [MASK] brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
encoding   = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')
input_ids  = encoding['input_ids']        # shape (1, T)

mask_pos   = (input_ids == tokenizer.mask_token_id).nonzero(as_tuple=True)[1]

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**encoding)
    logits  = outputs.logits               # (1, T, vocab_size)

# Top-5 predictions at the masked position
top5 = logits[0, mask_pos[0]].topk(5)
for score, idx in zip(top5.values, top5.indices):
    print(f"{tokenizer.decode([idx.item()]):<12} {score.item():.2f}")
# quick         11.43
# red           10.21
# little         9.87
# big            9.54
# gray           9.21
The loss is cross-entropy averaged over the masked positions only (using PyTorch's
is the set of masked positions and

or random tokens substituted). Because only ~15% of tokens are predicted per sequence, MLM sees about 6–7× fewer supervision signals per token than CLM, requiring longer training runs to match understanding quality.

import torch.nn as nn

def mlm_loss(logits, labels):
    """
    logits: (B, T, vocab_size)
    labels: (B, T)  — -100 at non-masked positions
    """
    loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(ignore_index=-100)
    # Flatten to (B*T, vocab) and (B*T,)
    return loss_fn(
        logits.view(-1, logits.size(-1)),
        labels.view(-1)
    )
Several models improved on BERT's original MLM setup:

— trains with dynamic masking (mask pattern regenerated each epoch rather than fixed at preprocessing), larger batches, and more data. Drops the Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) objective entirely, finding it unhelpful.
— replaces token-level MLM with Sentence Order Prediction (SOP) as the second objective; shares transformer layer weights across layers to reduce parameter count.
— a smaller model distilled from BERT-base using the MLM objective plus a cosine similarity loss on hidden states. Retains ~97% of BERT's performance at 60% the size.

— replaces MLM with a discriminative "replaced token detection" objective: a small generator fills masked positions, and the main model classifies every token as original or replaced. Trains on 100% of tokens, making it much more sample-efficient.
BERT's original masking operates at the subword (WordPiece) token level, which means it sometimes masks only part of a word — making the task trivial.
fixes this by masking all subword tokens that belong to the same word whenever any of them is selected.

# Illustrating the difference:
# Original: "The quick brown ##ish fox"
#
# Token-level masking (original BERT):
#   selected: idx 2 ("brown")  →  "The quick [MASK] ##ish fox"
#   model can trivially guess "brown" from "##ish" context
#
# Whole-word masking (WWM):
#   selected: idx 2 ("brown") — also masks idx 3 ("##ish")
#   →  "The quick [MASK] [MASK] fox"
#   model must understand context without any "brownish" clue

# In Hugging Face, enable WWM with DataCollatorForWholeWordMask:
from transformers import DataCollatorForWholeWordMask, BertTokenizer

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
collator  = DataCollatorForWholeWordMask(tokenizer, mlm_probability=0.15)
MLM vs Causal LM — when to use which:

Use MLM-pretrained encoders (BERT, RoBERTa) when your task involves reading and understanding text — sentiment, NER, extractive QA, semantic similarity. Use causal LM-pretrained decoders (GPT, LLaMA) when your task involves generating text — summarisation, chat, code completion, creative writing. Seq2Seq models (T5, BART) sit in between, encoding the input bidirectionally and decoding autoregressively.

Before BERT's MLM objective in 2018, the dominant pretraining approach was the left-to-right causal language model: predict the next word given all previous words. This causal constraint prevents each token from attending to its right context, producing representations that are inherently asymmetric and suboptimal for understanding tasks. Sentiment classification, named-entity recognition, and question answering all require full-sentence context — knowing what comes after a word is just as important as what came before.

MLM solved this by introducing an objective that is simultaneously self-supervised (no labels needed) and bidirectional. The result was a step change in performance across NLP benchmarks: BERT-base outperformed all previous state-of-the-art systems on GLUE at release, improving the benchmark average by over 7 points. MLM turned pretraining from a generative trick into a principled representation learning objective, and it remains the foundation of virtually every production language understanding system deployed today.
Every token attends to the full sentence. Representations capture left and right context simultaneously, which is essential for understanding tasks.
No manual labelling required. Masks are generated from existing text, allowing pretraining on massive unlabelled corpora (Wikipedia, Books).
MLM-pretrained models fine-tune rapidly on downstream tasks with as few as a few hundred labelled examples.
The random-token and unchanged-token components force the model to produce useful representations at every position, not just masked ones.
Continued pretraining with domain text (clinical notes, legal documents, code) on top of a general MLM checkpoint substantially improves in-domain performance.
Only 15% of tokens supervised per step:
Compared to CLM which predicts every token, MLM is ~6–7× less sample-efficient. Longer training or larger batches are required to match understanding quality.
The bidirectional attention and [MASK] tokens make encoder-only BERT models incapable of standard text generation. You need a decoder (GPT) or seq2seq (T5) for generation.

The [MASK] token appears during pretraining but never during fine-tuning or inference, creating a train-test distribution mismatch. The 10% random-token and 10% unchanged components partially mitigate this.
Most MLM models have a maximum sequence length (512 for BERT-base). Long documents require chunking strategies, losing cross-chunk context.
BERT-large training required 64 TPUs for 4 days. Continued pretraining from a checkpoint is far cheaper; training from scratch is not.

Use an MLM-pretrained encoder when: your task requires understanding rather than generation (sentiment analysis, NER, relation extraction, extractive QA, semantic similarity, text classification, coreference resolution). The task benefits from full-sentence bidirectional context, and outputs are fixed-size (classification labels, token-level tags, span boundaries).

Do not use MLM-only models when: you need to generate new text (summarisation, translation, dialogue, code completion). For those, use a causal LM (GPT, Claude) or a seq2seq model (T5, BART) which encodes bidirectionally but decodes autoregressively. If your application is a chatbot or content generator, CLM is the right pretraining objective.
Real-World Example: Fine-Tuning BERT for NER

Named-entity recognition (NER) is the canonical MLM fine-tuning task: the model must tag each token as PERSON, ORG, LOC, or O (outside). The bidirectional context from MLM pretraining is crucial — whether "Apple" means the company or the fruit depends on surrounding words on both sides. The following example fine-tunes bert-base-cased on a small NER dataset using Hugging Face Transformers.

from transformers import (
    BertTokenizerFast, BertForTokenClassification,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorForTokenClassification,
)
from datasets import Dataset
import numpy as np

# Toy NER data: word-level tags (B/I-PER, B/I-ORG, O)
label2id = {"O": 0, "B-PER": 1, "I-PER": 2, "B-ORG": 3, "I-ORG": 4}
id2label = {v: k for k, v in label2id.items()}

raw_train = [
    {"tokens": ["Elon", "Musk", "founded", "SpaceX", "."],
     "ner_tags": [1, 2, 0, 3, 0]},
    {"tokens": ["Tim", "Cook", "leads", "Apple", "Inc", "."],
     "ner_tags": [1, 2, 0, 3, 4, 0]},
]

tokenizer = BertTokenizerFast.from_pretrained("bert-base-cased")

def tokenize_and_align(example):
    enc = tokenizer(example["tokens"], is_split_into_words=True,
                    truncation=True, max_length=128)
    labels = []
    prev_word_id = None
    for word_id in enc.word_ids():
        if word_id is None:
            labels.append(-100)           # special tokens
        elif word_id != prev_word_id:
            labels.append(example["ner_tags"][word_id])
        else:
            # Continuation sub-word: use I- tag or -100 (common to use -100)
            labels.append(-100)
        prev_word_id = word_id
    enc["labels"] = labels
    return enc

dataset = Dataset.from_list(raw_train)
tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize_and_align, remove_columns=["tokens", "ner_tags"])

model = BertForTokenClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-cased",
    num_labels=len(label2id),
    id2label=id2label,
    label2id=label2id,
)

args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="/tmp/ner-bert",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    weight_decay=0.01,
    logging_steps=10,
    no_cuda=True,    # CPU for demo
    report_to="none",
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenized,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForTokenClassification(tokenizer),
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
)
trainer.train()
print("Training complete")
No autoregressive decoding capability; outputs garbage
Use GPT / T5 / BART for generation tasks
Fine-tuning with too high a learning rate
Use 1e-5 to 5e-5; warm up for first 10% of steps
Applying labels to sub-word continuation tokens
NER/token classification trained on wrong positions
Set label=-100 for continuation tokens; loss ignores them
Truncating long documents to 512 tokens blindly
Loses critical context from later parts of document
Use sliding window with overlap, or longformer/BigBird variants
Using uncased model for case-sensitive tasks
BERT-uncased lowercases input; NER loses capitalisation signal
Use bert-base-cased for NER and entity tasks
Never train a BERT-style model from scratch unless you have billions of tokens and significant compute. Use bert-base, roberta-base, or a domain-specific checkpoint.
If your domain differs from Wikipedia/Books, run a few thousand more MLM steps on domain text before fine-tuning. BioBERT for biomedical, LegalBERT for legal text — the pattern generalises.
DataCollatorForWholeWordMask prevents trivial sub-word reconstruction and produces better representations.
Fine-tune with a small learning rate and warmup:
2e-5 with 10% warmup steps and linear decay is a robust default across most classification and NER tasks.
MLM loss (cross-entropy) on a held-out domain sample is the right metric to track. Decreasing loss confirms the model is adapting.

BERT-base (110M parameters) requires roughly 3–4 GB GPU memory for inference at batch size 32. BERT-large (340M parameters) requires ~10 GB. For production inference, quantisation (INT8) reduces memory by 4× with less than 1% accuracy loss on most tasks. ONNX export plus runtime-specific kernels (TensorRT, ONNX Runtime) provide 2–4× inference speedup over raw PyTorch.

Fine-tuning BERT-base on a classification dataset with 10,000 examples takes 2–5 minutes on a single modern GPU (A100 or H100) for 3 epochs. The practical limit is sequence length — attention is O(n²) in sequence length, so 512-token inputs are 4× more expensive than 256-token inputs. DistilBERT and MobileBERT offer 2–4× speedups at ~95% of BERT accuracy for latency-constrained applications.
BertForMaskedLM, BertForSequenceClassification, BertForTokenClassification, DataCollatorForWholeWordMask — full MLM ecosystem.
Thousands of MLM checkpoints: bert-base-uncased, roberta-base, distilbert-base-uncased, microsoft/deberta-v3-base, and domain-specific variants (allenai/scibert, google/bigbird-roberta-base).
Wraps BERT-family models with pooling layers for sentence embeddings; optimised for semantic similarity and retrieval tasks.
Export BERT to ONNX for 2–4× faster CPU inference in production without GPU.
Simplifies distributed training of MLM pretraining across multiple GPUs or TPUs with minimal code changes.
Same as MLM but more sample-efficient

MLM-pretrained models inherit biases present in their pretraining corpus. BERT trained on English Wikipedia and Books corpus encodes gender, occupational, and cultural biases measurable with association tests (WEAT). Before deploying BERT-based classifiers in hiring, lending, or healthcare, evaluate for demographic bias using fairness toolkits such as Fairlearn or AI Fairness 360.

Reliability in production: BERT models are sensitive to input distribution shift. A model fine-tuned on customer reviews from 2022 may degrade on 2025 language if vocabulary or topic distributions have changed. Monitor classification confidence distributions over time; a drift toward low-confidence predictions signals the need for re-evaluation and possibly domain-adaptive retraining.
MLM masks 15% of tokens and trains the model to predict them from bidirectional context — no causal mask is applied.
The 80/10/10 split ([MASK] / random token / unchanged) prevents the model from only predicting at visible [MASK] positions.
Loss is computed only at masked positions; unmasked positions use label=-100 and contribute no gradient.
MLM is the right pretraining objective for understanding tasks (classification, NER, QA); causal LM is right for generation.
RoBERTa improved on BERT with dynamic masking, larger batches, and dropping NSP — eliminating one of BERT's two pretraining objectives.
Fine-tune with learning rate 1e-5 to 5e-5 and warmup; too high a rate causes catastrophic forgetting of pretrained weights.
ELECTRA's replaced-token detection predicts on 100% of tokens versus MLM's 15%, making it 4–5× more compute-efficient per step.
Whole-word masking prevents trivial sub-word reconstruction and yields better downstream performance with the same masking probability.
